title: L05 · Present Tense & Word Order
objective: Conjugate any regular German verb in the present tense across all six pronoun forms. Learn the V2 word-order rule that governs every main clause in German.
strategy: First memorise the six pronoun forms (ich, du, er/sie/es, wir, ihr, sie/Sie). Then drill one verb conjugation per minute by tapping each row. Section 4 covers V2 — the most important grammar rule in German.

sections.title: The six personal pronouns
sections.note: German has more pronoun distinctions than English. 'You' splits into 'du' (singular informal), 'ihr' (plural informal), and 'Sie' (formal, always capitalised).

blocks.hint: Always lowercase (unless it starts a sentence).
Use with one friend, family member, child, peer.

blocks.translation: he / she / it / él, ella, ello
blocks.hint: Used by grammatical gender, not natural gender. 'das Mädchen' → 'es' (because Mädchen is neuter).

blocks.translation: you all (plural, informal) / vosotros / ustedes informal
blocks.hint: Used when speaking to several friends/peers. Spanish: 'vosotros' (en España).

blocks.translation: they / you (formal, sing. or plur.) / ellos, ellas, usted, ustedes
blocks.hint: 'sie' (lowercase) = they. 'Sie' (CAPITAL) = formal 'you' — used with strangers, in business, with elders you don't know. Same conjugation as 'they'.

sections.title: Regular verb conjugation: the pattern
sections.note: Take the infinitive, drop -en (or -n), add personal endings. Drill the four sample verbs.

blocks.text: Endings: ich -e, du -st, er -t, wir -en, ihr -t, sie -en
blocks.audioText: machen: ich mache, du machst, er macht, wir machen, ihr macht, sie machen
blocks.rule: Take the stem (infinitive minus -en). Add: -e, -st, -t, -en, -t, -en. This pattern works for ~85% of German verbs.

examples.translation: I make the food. / Hago la comida.
What are you doing? / ¿Qué haces?
I live in Berlin. / Vivo en Berlín.
Where do you live? / ¿Dónde vives?
I'm learning German. / Aprendo alemán.
We're learning together. / Aprendemos juntos.
She plays piano. / Ella toca el piano.
We play football. / Jugamos al fútbol.

sections.title: Stem-changing verbs (the most common exceptions)
sections.note: Some verbs change their stem vowel in 'du' and 'er/sie/es' forms only. Wir, ihr, sie stay regular. These are HIGH-FREQUENCY verbs — memorise them.

blocks.audioText: sprechen, ich spreche, du sprichst, er spricht
blocks.rule: e → i in du and er/sie/es forms. Pattern shared with: helfen → hilfst/hilft, geben → gibst/gibt, essen → isst/isst.

examples.translation: I speak English and Spanish. / Hablo inglés y español.
You speak German very well. / Hablas alemán muy bien.

blocks.audioText: fahren, ich fahre, du fährst, er fährt
blocks.translation: to drive, to go (by vehicle) / ir (en vehículo), conducir
blocks.rule: a → ä in du and er/sie/es. Same group: schlafen → schläfst/schläft, tragen → trägst/trägt, laufen → läufst/läuft.

examples.translation: I'm going to Hamburg. / Voy a Hamburgo.
He drives a car. / Él conduce.

blocks.audioText: lesen, ich lese, du liest, er liest
blocks.rule: e → ie. Note: 'du liest' (not 'du liesst' — the s + st collapses). Same group: sehen → siehst/sieht.

examples.translation: I'm reading a book. / Leo un libro.
What are you reading? / ¿Qué lees?

sections.title: The V2 rule — the spine of German sentences
sections.note: In any main clause, the CONJUGATED VERB is the SECOND ELEMENT. The first element can be anything (subject, time, place, object) — but the verb must come in slot 2.

blocks.text: V2: conjugated verb sits in position 2
blocks.audioText: Ich trinke jetzt Kaffee. Jetzt trinke ich Kaffee. Kaffee trinke ich jetzt.
blocks.rule: Whatever you put first (subject, time word, place, even the object), the verb HAS to be next. Everything else fits afterwards. This is the most important word-order rule in German.

examples.translation: I drink coffee now. (Subject first) / Tomo café ahora.
Now I drink coffee. (Time first → subject AFTER verb) / Ahora tomo café.
Coffee is what I drink now. (Object first for emphasis) / Café es lo que tomo ahora.
Today I'm learning German. / Hoy aprendo alemán.
In Berlin many students live. / En Berlín viven muchos estudiantes.

blocks.text: Yes/no questions: VERB FIRST, subject second
blocks.audioText: Wohnst du in Berlin? Sprichst du Deutsch? Hast du Zeit?
blocks.rule: For yes/no questions, just invert: put the verb in position 1 and the subject in position 2.

examples.translation: Do you live in Berlin? / ¿Vives en Berlín?
Do you speak German? / ¿Hablas alemán?
Does he have a car? / ¿Tiene un coche?

blocks.text: W-questions: question word first, verb second, subject third
blocks.audioText: Wer bist du? Was machst du? Wo wohnst du? Wann kommst du? Warum lernst du Deutsch?
blocks.rule: Question words: WER (who), WAS (what), WO (where), WOHIN (where to), WOHER (where from), WANN (when), WARUM (why), WIE (how). They take slot 1, verb in slot 2.

examples.translation: Who are you? / ¿Quién eres?
What are you doing today? / ¿Qué haces hoy?
Where do you live? / ¿Dónde vives?
Where are you from? / ¿De dónde eres?
Where are you going? / ¿A dónde vas?
When does the film start? / ¿Cuándo empieza la película?
Why are you learning German? / ¿Por qué aprendes alemán?
What's your name? (lit. How are you called?) / ¿Cómo te llamas?
